cataloging_subject.label: The Wilton Chronicle
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English verse work composed for or in close association with the Benedictine convent at Wilton around 1420. It joins Wilton's institutional and royal history to the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith. The raw aje0623 XML represents Horstmann's title page and the poem on printed pp. 1-111, with empty page placeholders for omitted preliminaries and printed pp. 112-116. No Latin appendix content is encoded or counted as part of aje0623.

catalog_summary.title: The Wilton Chronicle
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Wilton poet
catalog_summary.date: Around 1420; sole surviving witness from the first quarter of the fifteenth century; first published in 1830
catalog_summary.region: Wilton, Wiltshire, England; an inferred South Wiltshire original is transmitted in a mixed-language copy made by a scribe associated with the West Midlands

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan CME item ID

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

assertion.notes: Current scholarship uses this concise title while recognizing that the work is both institutional history and hagiography.

scope.description: Descriptive title emphasizing the work's hagiographic center.

notes: Horstmann argued that Edith's legend, rather than annalistic history alone, is the poem's central purpose.

scope.description: Historical title used by Leland, Black, and later scholarship.

notes: This title should not imply that the text is merely a chronicle; its hagiographic and institutional-historical functions are inseparable.

titles.value: Almyȝty god in trinite, fader & sone and holy goste

titles.value: To þe same plase þer-as seynt Ede ys. Amen.

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor of the 1883 source edition represented by aje0623; not the medieval author.

scope.description: The Middle English verse work.

assertion.notes: The author had close knowledge of Wilton and probably wrote for its female religious community, but no personal name or secure clerical status is known.

notes: Older suggestions that the poet was one of Wilton's chaplains or that the sole copy was autograph remain hypotheses, not established authorship.
Benskin distinguishes authorial language from the extant copyist's mixed dialect and rejects a simple autograph model.

scope.description: Compilation and adaptation of hagiographic, historical, institutional, and local source material.
The modern title page, headings, transcription, punctuation, typography, and German notes represented in aje0623; omitted-page placeholders are separately recorded as representation parts.

date_statements.display: Around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V (1413-1422)

scope.description: Composition or final compilation of the Middle English Wilton Chronicle.

assertion.notes: Around 1420 is a scholarly inference supported by the complete Horstmann edition, the manuscript context, and modern scholarship. The adjacent Latin founders list used in that dating argument is not encoded as content in aje0623.

date_statements.display: First quarter of the fifteenth century (sole surviving witness)

scope.description: Date assigned to the Cotton Faustina B III component containing the work.

notes: The date belongs to the manuscript component and supports, but does not independently fix, composition around 1420.

scope.description: Likely place and institutional setting of composition.

assertion.notes: Composition at or for Wilton is strongly supported; the author's office, gender, and personal identity remain unknown.

scope.description: Benskin's reconstruction of the likely dialect of the work's originals.

assertion.notes: The extant manuscript language is mixed and predominantly West Midland; that scribal profile must not be flattened into the work's composition place.

scope.description: Broad cultural origin and historical horizon.

language_statements.label: Middle English

scope.description: The language of the Middle English verse work represented by 4,983 extant verbal verse rows within 4,988 raw L elements.

notes: The extant copy has mixed dialect features. Benskin separates the likely South Wiltshire originals from a copyist associated linguistically with northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably working at Wilton.
Short Latin quotations occur inside the Middle English poem, while German headings and notes belong to Horstmann's editorial frame; neither creates a Latin appendix part in aje0623.

form_statements.description: One Middle English narrative poem represented by 1,245 raw XML LG elements and 4,988 raw L elements; five L rows are non-verbal display or lacuna rows, 4,983 contain extant verbal verse, and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions.

scope.description: The abstract Wilton Chronicle.
Life, cult, relic translation, visions, and miracles of St Edith of Wilton.
A vernacular life and miracle collection centered on a female royal saint.
Chronologically organized history of Wilton, Wessex, royal founders, and the saint's cult integrated into the verse life.

assertion.notes: The label describes one major function and should not displace the work's hagiographic center.

scope.description: Vernacular devotional and institutional verse intended for a religious community and its wider audience.
Edith's life, sanctity, death, relics, posthumous appearances, miracles, and patronage of Wilton.

subject_statements.label: Wilton Abbey, its founders, and convent identity

scope.description: Institutional origins, benefactors, property, memory, devotional obligations, and historical identity of Wilton's female religious community.
Edith, Wulfthryth, Wilton's nuns, abbesses, visions, and the construction of female religious history.
Healing, punishment, visions, protection, and posthumous intercession.

subject_statements.label: Translation of relics and shrine devotion

scope.description: Edith's incorrupt body, elevation, shrine, relic protection, and later cult.
Royal succession, West Saxon history, Norman rule, and Wilton's place in English history.

summaries.value: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within an account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, royal patrons, and institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, shrine and relic miracles, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its female religious community. Its joined hagiography and history constructs a usable past for the abbey rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

summaries.value: The poem opens with English and West Saxon history and Wilton's founders, moves into Edgar's encounter with Wulfthryth, and then marks a twelve-folio manuscript loss after conventional line 1030. The surviving text resumes with Edith's upbringing, ascetic life, miracles, death, and first shrine cult. Horstmann later marks a second part beginning with the translation of Edith's relics; the remainder narrates royal patrons, punishments, healings, visions, and Edith's continuing protection of Wilton. The raw XML ends with the poem on printed p. 111. Its printed pp. 112-116 DIV1 is explicitly omitted back matter and contains only PB placeholders, so the adjacent Latin appendices are outside aje0623 content.

scope.description: The single surviving Middle English verse work represented by aje0623.

summaries.value: The raw header's LANGUAGE value ger reflects Horstmann's German editorial framing, not the target work's language. The header also places St Edith and Goscelin in author fields, but the medieval poem is anonymous and Edith is its subject. Current British Library foliation is five leaves later than Horstmann's legacy milestones: the poem is now ff. 199r-263r rather than ff. 194r-258r. Current f. 263r / IIIF canvas 535 contains the poem ending above and adjacent Latin below; only the poem portion belongs to aje0623.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary among abstract work, edition frame, physical witness, encoding, and adjacent content.

extent.description: Raw aje0623 structural axis: one BODY/DIV1 target work containing 1,245 LG and 4,988 L elements. Five L rows at raw positions 814, 1031, 1032, 1366, and 3520 are non-verbal lacuna/display rows. The remaining 4,983 L elements contain extant verbal verse. Three display rows stand for missing conventional positions and two are extra markers for the twelve-folio lacuna, so Horstmann's conventional running lineation has 4,986 positions. The title page and omitted front/back page placeholders are zero-count representation parts.

related_works.title: Legenda sanctae Edithae / Vita et translatio S. Edithae
notes: Modern scholarship treats the eleventh-century Edith legend associated with Goscelin as a principal source for the hagiographic material.
Horstmann doubted that the short twelve-chapter Bollandist Vita was identical to the Legenda named by the poet. The exact recension and physical exemplar therefore remain unresolved.

related_works.title: The Wilton Life of St Æthelthryth

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 265r-279v

notes: The companion Middle English verse life follows the Latin Wilton appendices in the same manuscript component and is generally assigned to the same anonymous author.
Benskin confirms close linguistic and scribal association while keeping author and copyist distinct.

evidence.citation: Local XML/XPath structural audit of aje0623.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 3 DIV1, 7 P, 124 PB, 125 EPB, 4,988 L, 1,245 LG, 364 NOTE1, 363 HI1, 64 folio MILESTONE, 4 GAP, and 2 ABBR elements. Body contains all 4,988 L and 1,245 LG plus 112 PB. LG cardinalities are 1,241 groups of four lines, two of five, one of six, and one of eight. Five L rows are non-verbal display/lacuna rows; 4,983 contain extant verbal verse and conventional lineation has 4,986 positions. The omitted-back DIV1 has only PB placeholders for printed pp. 112-116.

Binds the descriptive record to the raw AJE artifact, shared U-M item, later distinct phase-3 expression, Horstmann and Black editions, sole Cotton witness, complete facsimiles, DIMEV record, and scholarship.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. III-VIII; text pp. 1-113; remarks and Appendix II pp. 114-116

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199-280, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue record 041-001103881.
evidence.locator: ff. 199r-263r Life of St Edith; ff. 263r-264v Wilton chronicle; ff. 265r-279v Life of St Æthelthryth
evidence.summary: Current component description supporting the first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, Wilton origin, Middle English verse life locus, adjacent Latin Wilton text, companion Æthelthryth life, and bibliographic history.
notes: The component still carries a legacy statement that digital images are unavailable, while the parent record now provides working full IIIF coverage. The lineage manifest preserves this conflict explicitly.
Its bibliography prints Black's edition year as 1802; the inspected edition history and DIMEV support 1830.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the British Library IIIF sequence and complete local bundle for Cotton MS Faustina B III.
evidence.locator: target poem current ff. 199r-263r / IIIF canvases 407-535; f. 263r is a shared boundary page
evidence.summary: Visual inspection confirms the poem begins on f. 199r / canvas 407 and ends in the upper portion of f. 263r / canvas 535. Adjacent Latin begins below on the same final canvas but is outside aje0623 content.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 421, 'Almighty God in Trinity,' version 1.0.
evidence.summary: Identifies the poem as The Life of St Editha, supplies incipit and explicit, records the sole witness at current ff. 199-263, and cites Black 1830 and Horstmann 1883.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached DIMEV v1.0 source release.

evidence.citation: Michael Benskin, 'In reply to Dr Burton,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 22 (1991): 209-262.
evidence.locator: especially pp. 246-247 and the Part III analysis
evidence.summary: Distinguishes the South Wiltshire language of likely originals from the mixed, predominantly West Midland language of the extant copy; associates the scribe with northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably Wilton; confirms that the same hand copied the Edith poem, Latin account, and Æthelthryth life; and rejects simple autograph and author-dialect assumptions.
notes: The hash is for the complete locally cached 55-page article PDF.

evidence.citation: Brepols, Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth: Princesses, Miracle Workers, and their Late Medieval Audience (2009).
evidence.summary: Official publisher description for the modern edition and translation, conventional title The Wilton Chronicle, Wilton and early-fifteenth-century composition, female religious audience, subject framing, bibliographic details, and current purchase access.

evidence.citation: Mechthild Gretsch, review of Saints Edith and Æthelthryth, English Historical Review 126, no. 518 (2011): 121-122.
evidence.summary: Independent scholarly review describing the historical opening, the life after the manuscript lacuna, likely first-quarter-fifteenth-century composition, possible common authorship with the Æthelthryth poem, close Wilton connection, and the manuscript's probable production at Wilton around 1425.

evidence.citation: The Medieval Review, review of Mary Dockray-Miller, Saints Edith and Æthelthryth (2009).
evidence.summary: Summarizes the modern edition and its argument that the Wilton poet primarily adapted the eleventh-century Goscelin-associated Legenda Edithae to educate and entertain religious and lay women, promote Wilton, and combine history with hagiography.

evidence.citation: Cynthia Turner Camp, 'Edith of Wilton and the Writing of Women's History,' in Anglo-Saxon Saints' Lives as History Writing in Late Medieval England (Boydell & Brewer, 2015), 25-63.
evidence.summary: Scholarly chapter abstract framing the poem as an anonymous work composed around 1420 for an identifiable female community, reconstructing Wilton's pre-Conquest past through joined hagiographic and historical writing.

open_questions.question: Was the anonymous Wilton poet a chaplain, another male cleric, a nun, or another member of the abbey's textual community?
next_steps: Retain anonymous authorship unless documentary or paleographic evidence identifies a person.
Consult the full argument and notes in Dockray-Miller and later scholarship for any post-2009 reassessment of gender or office.
notes: Close institutional affiliation is well supported; personal identity and profession are not.

open_questions.question: Which recension or physical exemplar of the Legenda sanctae Edithae underlies the poem, and how exactly is it related to the short Goscelin-associated Vita printed by the Bollandists?
next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's source notes against Horstmann's objections and current Goscelin scholarship.
Do not convert the Auctoritates list into a claim for an identified physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: What was the exact text and line count of the twelve-folio gathering lost after surviving line 1030?
next_steps: Compare source analogues and later summaries without presenting reconstruction as surviving text.
Seek physical or codicological evidence for when the gathering was removed.
notes: Horstmann's approximately 980 missing lines is an estimate and is excluded from the surviving verse count.

open_questions.question: What exact source or editorial function does each of aje0623's 363 HI1 spans serve, and how should they be compared with the later phase-3 file's 6,738 HI spans?
next_steps: Collate aje0623's 354 superscript and 9 italic HI1 spans against Horstmann's page images and the manuscript.
Compare the later phase-3 revision without importing its denser markup or Latin content into aje0623.
notes: The raw OCR/Level-1 file and later proofed phase-3 expression encode typography at materially different granularities.

open_questions.question: Can the complete historical set of 124 U-M-linked page images be retrieved and audited through the current item interface?
next_steps: Retry the current View Page routes in an interactive browser or through U-M-supported access.
Do not describe the linked image set as currently complete or unavailable until all sequences are checked.
notes: The current full-text page is public, but automated page-view requests returned HTTP 403 during this audit.
The U-M catalog places St Edith in an author position, but she is the poem subject; Goscelin is a possible source-author association, not the anonymous Middle English author.
The preferred title follows current scholarship. Chronicon Vilodunense and The Life of St Edith remain searchable alternates.
The target is one verse work in one witness. Raw LG groups are XML stanza containers, not separately cataloged works.
The title page, German apparatus, and omitted-page placeholders are zero-count representation parts.
The Latin appendices on printed pp. 111-116 remain edition/manuscript context only and are completely outside aje0623 target content and arithmetic.
The raw structural counts, verbal-line count, and conventional line positions are deliberately reported separately.
The surviving manuscript copy is not treated as autograph. Benskin's analysis separates original language, scribal language, place of composition, and place of copying.
Current foliation governs discovery and facsimile navigation; Horstmann's old foliation and the raw XML's corrupt milestone sequence remain representation evidence.
The complete British Library IIIF bundle and complete Horstmann volume are cached; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the source objects.
The phase-3 CME00147 artifact is a later proofed, restructured, and expanded repository expression related by supported version_of, never same_as.
